1.synthesis: Psalm 136 is the “Great Hallel,” distinguished from every other psalm by its antiphonal structure: every verse ends with the repeated refrain “for his steadfast love endures forever” (ki le’olam hasdo). The opening section summons praise for YHWH’s essential character and creation: “Give thanks to the Lord, for he is good, for his steadfast love endures forever. Give thanks to the God of gods, for his steadfast love endures forever. Give thanks to the Lord of lords, for his steadfast love endures forever” (Ps 136:1–3). This triple opening—Lord, God of gods, Lord of lords—establishes YHWH’s supremacy before the psalm descends into its specific demonstrations. The creation section follows: “to him who alone does great wonders… to him who made the heavens with understanding… who spread out the earth above the waters… who made the great lights… the sun to rule over the day… the moon and stars to rule over the night” (136:4–9), each element punctuated by the undying refrain.
1.historical_context: The antiphonal structure of Psalm 136 suggests liturgical performance in which a cantor or priest sang the narrative line and the congregation or choir responded with the refrain. The repetition of “for his steadfast love endures forever” twenty-six times in twenty-six verses is not rhetorical excess but the structural embodiment of the psalm’s theological claim: every act of YHWH in creation and history is an expression of the same underlying steadfast love that is his permanent character. The opening section’s emphasis on creation aligns Psalm 136 with Genesis 1, whose days of creation are reflected in the enumeration of heavenly bodies and the ordering of the earth. The creation acts are then the foundation for the historical acts that follow: the same steadfast love that made the sun to rule over the day sent the plagues of Egypt and led Israel through the wilderness.
1.literary_structure: Psalm 136 is organized into four sections distinguished by their content: creation (vv. 1–9), Exodus (vv. 10–16), conquest (vv. 17–22), and thanksgiving (vv. 23–26). The first section establishes the template for what follows: YHWH’s acts in creation are presented not as distant prehistory but as ongoing expressions of the steadfast love that was also active in history and is accessible to the worshipping community. The three titles in verses 1–3 (Lord, God of gods, Lord of lords) create a crescendo of superlatives before the specific acts begin—each act then becomes an instance of what it means to be God of gods and Lord of lords. The final verse (“Give thanks to the God of heaven,” v. 26) echoes the opening and closes the psalm as an envelope, so that the psalm begins and ends in the same posture of grateful acknowledgment.
1.theological_application: The antiphonal refrain of Psalm 136 performs a kind of liturgical formation: to say twenty-six times in the course of a single worship gathering that “his steadfast love endures forever” is to rehearse a conviction until it becomes habitual. The specific virtue being habituated is the connection between particular events and the underlying permanent character of YHWH: each event (creation, Exodus, conquest, present salvation) is not a one-time exception but an expression of the love that always was and always will be. Communities shaped by Psalm 136 learn to read every event in their history—both personal and corporate—through the lens of the refrain: this too is an expression of the steadfast love that endures forever. The universality of the claim (creation, history, present redemption, and everything in between) leaves no domain outside the scope of hesed.
1.christology: The three titles of verses 1–3—Lord, God of gods, Lord of lords—are applied in the New Testament to Christ: “he is Lord of lords and King of kings” (Rev 17:14). The steadfast love that endures forever finds its ultimate expression in Christ: “God shows his love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us” (Rom 5:8)—the hesed of the psalm is the love that sent the Son. The creation acts attributed to YHWH’s steadfast love in this section are the acts of Christ the agent of creation: “by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible” (Col 1:16). In Christ, the refrain achieves its fullest meaning: the love that made the sun and the moon is the love that hung on the cross, and it endures forever.

10.synthesis: The second section of Psalm 136 moves from creation to the Exodus, presenting YHWH’s acts of deliverance with the same rhythmic acknowledgment that each arises from steadfast love: “to him who struck down the firstborn of Egypt, for his steadfast love endures forever; and brought Israel out from among them, for his steadfast love endures forever; with a strong hand and an outstretched arm, for his steadfast love endures forever; to him who divided the Red Sea in two, for his steadfast love endures forever; and made Israel pass through the midst of it, for his steadfast love endures forever; but overthrew Pharaoh and his host in the Red Sea, for his steadfast love endures forever; to him who led his people through the wilderness, for his steadfast love endures forever” (Ps 136:10–16). Seven acts of the Exodus are each attributed to the steadfast love that endures forever, so that the liberation of Israel is presented not as a historical accident but as the temporal shape of YHWH’s eternal character.
10.historical_context: The Exodus acts enumerated in this section correspond to the major events of Exodus 1–15: the plagues (represented by the striking of the firstborn), the departure from Egypt, the Red Sea crossing and Pharaoh’s destruction, and the wilderness journey. The “strong hand and outstretched arm” (136:12) is the Deuteronomic formula for YHWH’s Exodus intervention (Deut 4:34; 5:15; 26:8). The wilderness journey (136:16) is the bridge between the dramatic Exodus events and the conquest of the land that follows: YHWH’s leading through the wilderness is also an act of steadfast love, even in its ambiguity. The antiphonal refrain attaches the same unconditional declaration to the wilderness journey as to the Red Sea crossing, refusing to grade the events of redemptive history by their dramatic intensity.
10.literary_structure: The seven Exodus acts follow a narrative arc that begins with judgment against Egypt (striking the firstborn), moves through liberation (bringing Israel out), divine power (strong hand and outstretched arm), miraculous deliverance (Red Sea divided and passed through), judgment against Pharaoh (overthrown in the Sea), and ongoing care (wilderness leading). The accumulation of seven acts with seven repetitions of the refrain creates a liturgical reenactment: the congregation that sings the refrain is drawn into the original community’s experience of deliverance, so that each “for his steadfast love endures forever” is both a historical acknowledgment and a present profession of trust in the same love that is still active on their behalf.
10.theological_application: The attribution of the Exodus acts to YHWH’s steadfast love (hesed) is theologically precise: the Exodus was not merely a display of power (“he is stronger than Pharaoh”) but an expression of covenantal character (“he is faithful to his promise to Abraham”). Communities shaped by this section learn to read their own stories of deliverance as evidence of the same steadfast love, not merely of circumstantial good fortune or divine caprice. The wilderness journey’s inclusion in the list of love-acts (136:16) is pastorally important: the forty years of wandering, with their difficulties and failures, were not outside the sphere of YHWH’s steadfast love but inside it. The psalm refuses to confine hesed to the dramatic moments of crossing and conquest.
10.christology: The Exodus deliverance is the Old Testament type of the salvation Christ accomplishes: Paul explicitly identifies the Red Sea crossing as a baptism into Moses that is fulfilled in baptism into Christ (1 Cor 10:1–4). The wilderness journey of verse 16 points to Christ who was led by the Spirit into the wilderness (Matt 4:1) and who is the bread from heaven that sustained Israel and sustains the church (John 6:35). The overthrow of Pharaoh in the Red Sea (136:15) is the type of the overthrow of the powers in the death of Christ: “he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him” (Col 2:15). Each Exodus act refracted through the refrain becomes an anticipation of the greater exodus Christ accomplishes.

17.synthesis: The third section of Psalm 136 moves from Exodus to conquest, continuing the antiphonal catalog of YHWH’s acts: “to him who struck down great kings, for his steadfast love endures forever; and killed mighty kings, for his steadfast love endures forever; Sihon, king of the Amorites, for his steadfast love endures forever; and Og, king of Bashan, for his steadfast love endures forever; and gave their land as a heritage, for his steadfast love endures forever; a heritage to Israel his servant, for his steadfast love endures forever” (Ps 136:17–22). The specific naming of Sihon and Og (136:19–20) grounds the praise in concrete history rather than generality. The section culminates in the gift of land as heritage, which is the covenant’s long-promised content: what YHWH’s steadfast love began in the deliverance from Egypt reaches its first fulfillment in the settlement of the promised land.
17.historical_context: Sihon king of the Amorites and Og king of Bashan (136:19–20) are the two kings defeated east of the Jordan before Israel crossed into Canaan, as narrated in Numbers 21:21–35 and retold in Deuteronomy 2:26–3:11. They represent the beginning of the conquest pattern—the first kings to fall before YHWH’s advancing people—and their defeat is cited in Psalm 135, 136, and throughout Deuteronomy as the archetypal demonstration that YHWH fights for Israel. The land as “heritage” (136:21) uses the same word (nachalah) that Psalm 127 applied to children and Psalm 135 applied to the land: in each case, the gift is not earned but received as the inheritance given by YHWH to his servant people.
17.literary_structure: The conquest section follows the same pattern as the Exodus section: YHWH’s acts are enumerated with increasing specificity (great kings in general, then mighty kings, then Sihon and Og by name) before arriving at the concluding gift. The progression from general to named is the psalm’s way of insisting that the steadfast love it praises is not abstract but historically specific: it attached to particular kings on particular days in identifiable places. The land-heritage at the close of this section (vv. 21–22) functions as a literary landing point that makes the conquest the goal toward which both the Exodus and wilderness sections were aimed—the steadfast love was always moving Israel toward an inheritance.
17.theological_application: The conquest section of Psalm 136 presents one of the most morally challenging aspects of Israel’s history—the defeat and displacement of Canaanite peoples—within the framework of steadfast love and thanksgiving. Communities shaped by this section must grapple with the psalm’s refusal to separate YHWH’s love for Israel from the judgment that falls on the nations that opposed his people’s inheritance. The theological claim is that YHWH’s steadfast love to his people necessarily involves action against those who resist his purposes—the same character that delivers Israel acts in judgment against those who would prevent that deliverance. The inheritance given to Israel as YHWH’s servant (136:22) is the covenant’s fulfillment, the content of the promise made to Abraham that YHWH’s steadfast love has been working to fulfill since the beginning.
17.christology: The defeat of Sihon and Og as “mighty kings” (136:17–20) points to Christ as the one who defeats the ultimate powers. Paul identifies the conquest pattern with the victory of Christ over the spiritual rulers and authorities: “he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet” (1 Cor 15:25). The land-heritage given to Israel (136:21) is the type of the inheritance given in Christ to all who are Abraham’s heirs by faith: “the promise to Abraham and his offspring that he would be heir of the world did not come through the law but through the righteousness of faith” (Rom 4:13). The steadfast love that gave land to Israel is the love that gives the whole creation as inheritance to those who are in Christ—an enlargement that Psalm 136’s refrain always implied.

23.synthesis: The final section of Psalm 136 makes explicit what the whole psalm has assumed: YHWH’s steadfast love is active not only in the ancient events of creation, Exodus, and conquest but in the present experience of the worshipping community: “It is he who remembered us in our low estate, for his steadfast love endures forever; and rescued us from our foes, for his steadfast love endures forever; he who gives food to all flesh, for his steadfast love endures forever. Give thanks to the God of heaven, for his steadfast love endures forever” (Ps 136:23–26). The shift from third person narrative (“him who struck down”) to first person community experience (“remembered us”) marks the psalm’s movement from historical recital to present testimony. The inclusion of “all flesh” in verse 25 universalizes the psalm’s scope—the steadfast love that chose Israel and delivered them from Egypt is the same love that gives food to every living creature.
23.historical_context: The “low estate” and rescue from foes in verses 23–24 may reflect the post-exilic context of the psalm’s use, when the community’s experience of Babylonian captivity and restoration was the present equivalent of the Exodus. The “God of heaven” (136:26) is a title that appears prominently in the Persian-period books of Ezra, Nehemiah, and Daniel, where it served as the universal designation for Israel’s God in a multicultural imperial context. The provision of food for “all flesh” (136:25) grounds the universalism of YHWH’s care: the steadfast love that is celebrated in Israel’s particular story is also operative in the sustaining of all living creatures, connecting Psalm 136 to the creation theology of Genesis and Psalm 104.
23.literary_structure: The final section of Psalm 136 performs the psalm’s essential movement in miniature: from the remembered past (“he who remembered us,” v. 23) to the contemporary (“rescued us from our foes”) to the universal (“gives food to all flesh”) and back to the act of thanksgiving (v. 26). The psalm’s closing verse echoes its opening (“give thanks to the Lord, for he is good”), creating a structure in which the whole history of YHWH’s acts is enclosed within the community’s act of gratitude. The final title (“God of heaven”) elevates the thanksgiving to cosmic scale: the community that praises is praising not a tribal deity but the God of the entire cosmos, whose steadfast love spans creation and history alike.
23.theological_application: The shift to “remembered us” (136:23) is the psalm’s most important move for communal formation: it invites the worshipping community to claim the history of YHWH’s acts as their own story, not merely as the past of their ancestors. The community that sings Psalm 136 is saying, “This is what happened to us”—taking ownership of the Exodus, the wilderness, the conquest, and the remembered rescue as their corporate experience. The provision of food for all flesh (136:25) prevents the community from making their election the terminus of YHWH’s love: his steadfast love extends beyond his chosen people to sustain every living creature. The final thanksgiving thus has a universalizing humility: the community praises not the God who loves only them but the God of heaven whose love endures forever toward all.
23.christology: “It is he who remembered us in our low estate” (136:23) is the movement of the Incarnation: the God of heaven remembered humanity in its fallen low estate and entered it. Paul describes this in the language of descent: “though he was in the form of God, did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself, by taking the form of a servant, being born in the likeness of men” (Phil 2:6–7). The food given to all flesh (136:25) reaches its christological concentration in Christ as bread of life: “I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger” (John 6:35)—the one who provides food for all flesh is himself the food that gives life to the world. The final thanks to the “God of heaven” is the thanks of the church whose Lord ascended to the right hand of the Father, the God of heaven who is also Emmanuel.
